An snapshot of salary research for prospect dev roles curated from LinkedIn, public databases, and more.

***

![](cash2.jpg){width=50%}

# Overview
This is exactly what it sounds like: a little snapshot of industry data, independently curated. I'm not trying to compete with the [Apra International Salary Survey](https://aprahome.org/Resources/Salary-Survey){target="_blank"}. Actually this part 1 post is more about how I'm gathering the data, and is more proof of concept, rather than readable report. I'm trying to document my work as I go along.

#### The Good News
The data I have gathered is free to explore, though it is missing many useful columns at the moment. I have over 1700 individual salary observations from 2012 to 2026, which is not bad, considering I'm maybe 10% of the way through my identified potential sources. 

{{< downloadthis prospdev_highered_salaries_2026-04-11.csv dname=ProspDev-HigherEdSalaries label="Download the higher ed prospect dev salary data .csv" >}}

#### The Bad News
Big shocker: you probably won't get rich as a prospect development professional. However, it does seem like there is more stability in the higher education sector, similar to government jobs (at least, historically), compared to other types of analysts within industry. It feels like Business Intelligence analysts are often on the chopping block for layoffs because they are seen as helpful, but non-essential. I see really talented people on LinkedIn with much better resumes than me suddenly needing a new gig. I guess the trick is to get into management, not just analysis, or else continue to be a sacrificial lamb for the sake of an IPO, or whatever. I digress.

If you want stronger methodology, more data across sectors beyond higher education, broader scope, and mild abuse of the "3d" effect for bar charts...you'll have to wait until the next Apra Salary Survey comes out in 2027.[^paywall] I limited myself to public information, which as any prospect researcher will tell you, can sometimes be prone to massive gaps.

The major highlight so far has been seeing all the different job titles, which will help with future research. My current process is not very good at capturing "multiple hat" roles where prospect development is only one part of the job - and I'm thinking I can use some natural language processing to help me with that categorization. Considering I'm relying heavily on "prospect" as a keyword, many of the multiple hat roles - sometimes with vague titles like "development coordinator" - are being missed.

The smaller the shop, the more likely you are to wear multiple hats: database admin, gift processing, etc. Major gift officers also do a plenty of prospect research, independent of support staff. 

# The Prospect Dev Salary Dataset
The first attempt at cobbling this dataset together yielded:


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 5
  unique_titles min_salary max_salary unique_states total_records
          <int>      <dbl>      <dbl>         <int>         <dbl>
1            71          0     256595            16          1753
```


:::
:::




***
![](coins.jpg){width=50%}

# Additional Methodology

## Salary Sourcing & Pre-processing

### Bulk Data
I made a data request to the National Labor Exchange (NLx) Research Hub, to try and get access to a larger corpus of online job adds, occupation codes, tools, salaries, etc. However, I don't think my research case is as compelling as the guys at Loyola who trained an [entire model](https://github.com/Job-Ad-Research-at-QSB-LUC/JAAT) on billions of data points to make very slick job analysis modules in Python. Luckily, their Job Ad Analysis Toolkit will help me with my unstructured text problem - which has been building up as I grab job descriptions and other data from LinkedIn. However, their giant corpus, as far as I know, is under an agreement with NLx that may specify what they can or can't publish. 

I need the job-level data so I can filter for prospect dev roles, and I'm tempted to email them to ask for a subset of their corpus, but again, the NLx agreement may prevent that.

National bulk data for prospect dev roles remains out of reach, for now, so I had to do a state-by-state approach, starting with large public universities.

### Large Public Universities
Some notes below about the data aggregation process for states where I dug deeper than national websites like OpenGovPay.com - which seem to have pretty major gaps. Those gaps were what drove me to state-level websites in the first place. When I ran into years where I knew for certain there should be salary data, because the development department existed and had prospect development professionals on the payroll, but the data didn't fully filter into whatever source those websites use. 


#### California
**Data:** [University of California compensation](https://ucannualwage.ucop.edu/wage/)

**Years:** 2017-2024

**Note:** searched by title, used "Prospect", there may be other key words to use or job titles, arguably more data in there - especially since digital records go back to 2010, just need to find the right keywords
Problems: This is the first time I'm seeing Gross Pay vs. Regular pay vs. overtime pay vs. other pay. I have realized I am actually not 100% sure about whether some of my other data is gross, regular, or some mysterious aggregate. Generally speaking, I'm always taking the lower number, with no extras (e.g. benefits) for this version of the dataset. I'm still saving all the other data, but don't want to bother with it yet. California has one of the larger fundraising worker populations in the country, and is a great example of why cost of living analyses will be important to compare across states and get more accurate averages. Their higher pay will drive up the mean and make the salaries across the country seem rosier than they actually are.

**Job titles:** Prospect Rsch Spec 1-5. Presumably "Prospect Research Specialist"

#### Utah
**Data:** [Transparent Utah data dashboard and downloads](https://old.transparent.utah.gov/entity_comp.php)

**Years:** 2014-2026

**Note:** I wish the data went back farther. It seems the mid-2010s is a common starting point for these state-level data dashboards. Older salary data is locked within PDFs, which may be public, but hard to find. Once you hit the mid-2000s and before, some of the data may not be digitized, and exist within "special collections" in libraries that you have to be in-person to access. 

#### Indiana
**Data:** [Indiana gateway](https://gateway.ifionline.org/report_builder/)

**Years:** 2012-2025

**Note:** I'm sure there is a better way to do this, but I just went university by university in the "Search by Unit" tab. Filtering can be annoying, because sometimes the name of the division changes in the documents halfway through the year range. Still, with 13 years worth of data across several Indiana universities, this will be useful for longer time-frame analyses.

#### Wyoming
**Data:** Various fiscal reports

**Years:** 2018-2026

**Note:** There's got to be a data dashboard somewhere, I found a bunch of PDFs with salary schedules, but it was unclear to my untrained eye if that data was actual expenditures, or just what they were approved to list as the salaries? In any case, it took too long to retrieve only a little data, so I really want the National Labor Exchange to respond to my request. Or see what I can do about finding a better PDF data extraction tool. ChatGPT does alright, but it's still full of mistakes. OCR is always a pain...

***

### Ad Hoc Job Listings
I have several job alerts on LinkedIn for prospect development related roles, but data is also coming from Indeed, Regional Apra Job postings, etc. For now, I'm only including the bottom of the range for salaries, but these are fresh listings - so the data is from 2025-2026. I add to this list every week or so, but my workflow leaves something to be desired.

I'm mostly tracking these to capture skills and experience levels.

***

# Next steps
- Switching to a database workflow, where the .csv files are just for spot checking or visualization snapshots. 
- Get all states represented
- Use the Job Ad Analysis Toolkit to make heirarchies based on job title, or work on developing a heirarchy methodology
- Adding very basic cost of living estimates at the state (not county) level. Better than nothing.
- Use a linear regression (or something?) to make estimates for same-title roles in years that are missing. So if I have "Prospect Research Analyst" in Utah for 2012, 2014, 2015, etc... but I'm missing 2013, I'd like to try and fill in that gap. Estimates will always be labeled. This will make for an interesting expanded dataset.
- Adding these steps to the private Toolkit project on Github

# My reading list to learn more
- [Performance-Based Salary Distribution in Universities Using Extreme Gradient Boosting with Particle Swarm Optimization](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/11341436)
- [Education vs. Earnings: An Analytical Perspective on Fields of Study and Salaries Using Python](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-95652-2_31)
- [Extracting O*NET Features from the NLx Corpus to Build Public Use Aggregate Labor Market Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.01470)
- Subhani, S., Memon, S.A. & AlShebli, B. CMap: a database for mapping job titles, sector specialization, and promotions across 24 sectors. Sci Data 12, 1214 (2025). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-025-05526-3

***

[^paywall]: I'm also curious who actually pays the $275 non-member fee to access the salary survey...which seems too steep to be realistic as an actual product people would buy. I'm guessing that number was chosen to make the Professional Membership benefits seem all that more attractive, a [psychological pricing technique](https://www.advergize.com/glossary/psychological-pricing/){target="_blank"} that nudges users toward the ongoing subscription. Presumably, though, there are some folks in prospect dev who don't have access to the Professional Membership, which is another reason to have independent and free data for all.
